md: Two lines on a plane can have one of four relationships: they're the **same line**, they're **parallel** (never meet), they're **perpendicular** (meet at a right angle), or they're **none of the above** (they meet at some other angle).
What's beautiful: you can tell which case you're in just by looking at the **slopes**.
Parallel lines have *exactly the same slope*. They tilt the same way, just at different vertical positions:

blocks.tex: L_1 \parallel L_2 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; m_1 = m_2 \;\text{ and }\; b_1 \neq b_2

md: If the slopes were equal *and* the y-intercepts were equal, they'd be the same line.
Try it below: set $m_1 = m_2$ but make $b_1 \neq b_2$. The relationship indicator should say **Parallel**.
Two lines are perpendicular when their slopes multiply to $-1$:
Equivalently, $m_2 = -\dfrac{1}{m_1}$. We say $m_2$ is the **negative reciprocal** of $m_1$.

blocks.title: Quick mental shortcut
blocks.body: To find the slope of a perpendicular line: **flip the fraction and negate**. So $m = \tfrac{3}{4}$ gives $m_\perp = -\tfrac{4}{3}$. That's the whole rule.

md: Horizontal lines have $m = 0$; vertical lines have undefined slope. They're perpendicular to each other — the *one* perpendicular pair our formula can't directly verify (you can't multiply something by undefined). Just remember it as a special case.
**Find the equation of the line through $(2, 1)$ that is perpendicular to $y = -3x + 5$.**

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: **Parallel** ↔ $m_1 = m_2$ (same slope, different y-intercepts). **Perpendicular** ↔ $m_1 \cdot m_2 = -1$ (negative reciprocals). Same line ↔ $m_1 = m_2$ AND $b_1 = b_2$.
